critic_raw:
The churn-model evaluation has a clear and consequential metric misalignment.

**Issue 1 (global_auc_not_decision_metric):** ROC-AUC is a threshold-independent summary over the full score distribution. However, the retention team operates exclusively in the top 2% of the scored population — they can only call 2% of users per week. AUC gains in the middle and bottom of the distribution (which may constitute the majority of the AUC improvement) are operationally irrelevant. A model could improve AUC substantially by better separating low-risk users from each other, with no improvement at all in the top 2%. The paper uses AUC as a proxy for top-2% ranking quality, but this is not guaranteed.

**Issue 2 (no_capacity_constrained_evaluation):** The paper does not report precision in the top 2%, recall in the top 2% (i.e., what fraction of actual churners are captured in the top 2%), or lift at the top 2%. These are the metrics that directly measure the model's utility for the actual retention workflow. Without them, there is no evidence that the AUC improvement translates to a better list of 2% users to call.

**Issue 3 (prediction_metric_not_intervention_metric):** Even if the new model produces a better top-2% list, the paper does not report incremental retention rate among contacted users. The business outcome is not 'rank users better' — it is 'retain more users.' A better-ranked list is only valuable if contacting the identified users actually reduces churn. This metric is absent.

**Data science team rebuttal assessment:** The argument that AUC is 'threshold independent and therefore more robust' is technically correct as a description of AUC, but irrelevant to the decision at hand. The business problem is inherently threshold-dependent (exactly 2% capacity constraint). Threshold-independence is a weakness here, not a strength — the model is being used at exactly one operating point.
defender_raw:
Defense of the churn model evaluation:

**Point 1 — AUC is the right metric for ranking quality:** ROC-AUC measures the model's global discrimination ability. A model with higher AUC is provably better at ranking churners above non-churners across all possible thresholds. This is a stronger guarantee than any single top-k metric, which is sensitive to the specific threshold chosen and to the particular distribution of scores in a test set.

**Point 2 — The practical implication of higher AUC:** In practice, a model with higher AUC will generally produce better top-k lists. The relationship between AUC improvement and top-k improvement is imperfect but reliably positive in most empirical evaluations. The 0.84 vs 0.77 gap is substantial — a 7-point AUC improvement rarely fails to produce meaningful gains at any reasonable operating point.

**Point 3 — Monthly recalibration mitigates threshold concerns:** The model is recalibrated monthly. This ensures that score distributions remain calibrated over time, which means top-k performance is likely to remain stable as the population changes.

**Point 4 — Call script invariance simplifies the evaluation:** The unchanged call script and offer menu mean that any improvement in the model's output list directly corresponds to improvement in retention outcomes, holding the intervention constant. This is actually a favorable feature of the evaluation design.
adjudication_raw:
The critic identifies a real and important metric misalignment. The business constraint — 2% weekly capacity — means the model will always be used at one operating point. AUC measures performance across all operating points. The defender's argument that higher AUC generally implies better top-k performance is an empirical generalization, not a guarantee, and 'generally positive' is not sufficient justification for a workflow change.

The defender's strongest point is that a 7-point AUC improvement is large. However, AUC can improve due to better separation anywhere in the distribution — including areas that are irrelevant to the top 2% cutoff. The paper provides no evidence that the improvement manifests at the operational threshold.

The intervention metric point (issue 3) is also unaddressed by the defense. The call script being unchanged does not mean contacted users are retained — it simply holds one variable constant. Incremental retention among called users is still unmeasured.

The core question — does the AUC improvement translate to a better top-2% list and better retention outcomes — is empirically testable with data already available (the test month data should include records of which users were called and whether they churned).

Verdict: empirical_test_agreed
Measure top-2% precision, recall, and lift for both models on the held-out month; report actual retention rates among called users to resolve whether the AUC gain matters at the operational threshold.

empirical_test.measure: precision and lift in the top 2% of scored users on the held-out test month
empirical_test.success_criterion: new model shows higher precision at top-2% cutoff and higher incremental retention among contacted users than the incumbent logistic model
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top-2% precision for new model is not statistically different from incumbent, or incremental retention is not improved, indicating the AUC gain did not manifest at the operational threshold
